quiz.title: SAT: Unit 2 Quiz 7
quiz.subtitle: Identifying Evidence That Supports or Undermines an Argument (10 Questions)
quiz.subject: SAT Preparation

questions.question:
Ecologists claim that restoring wetland habitats reduces coastal flooding damage primarily by absorbing storm surge, not by acting as a physical barrier to wind.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Site Type} & \text{Storm Surge Absorption (%)} & \text{Wind Speed Reduction (%)} \\ \hline\text{Restored Wetland} & 68 & 12 \\ \hline\text{Natural Wetland} & 74 & 15 \\ \hline\text{Developed Coastline} & 8 & 9 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which finding, if true, would most strengthen the ecologists' claim?
options: Developed coastlines show slightly higher wind reduction than restored wetlands in some storms.
Coastal communities report subjective perceptions of safety after wetland restoration projects.
Restored and natural wetlands absorb most storm surge while reducing wind speed minimally.
Wetland plants grow more rapidly in regions with higher annual rainfall and milder temperatures.
questions.explanation: Choice C directly supports the claim by showing high surge absorption paired with low wind reduction, matching the proposed mechanism. Choice A focuses on a minor comparison that doesn't address the core mechanism. Choice B cites subjective perceptions, not physical evidence. Choice D introduces plant growth rates, irrelevant to flood mitigation.

questions.question:
Economists propose that raising the minimum wage increases consumer spending in low-income neighborhoods but has negligible effects on small-business employment levels.

Which finding, if true, would most weaken this proposal?
options: Consumer spending on essential goods rose by 8% in neighborhoods following minimum wage increases.
Large corporations adjusted pricing strategies more readily than small businesses after wage changes.
Small businesses in regions with higher minimum wages reduced staff hours by 15% on average.
Low-income households allocate a larger share of income to housing and transportation costs.
questions.explanation: Choice C directly challenges the 'negligible employment effects' claim by showing measurable staff-hour reductions. Choice A supports the consumer-spending part of the proposal. Choice B compares business types without addressing employment outcomes. Choice D describes spending patterns, not wage-policy impacts.

questions.question:
A cognitive psychologist hypothesizes that spaced repetition improves long-term retention not by increasing total study time, but by optimizing the timing of memory reconsolidation.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Study Schedule} & \text{Total Time (min)} & \text{Retention Score (%)} \\ \hline\text{Massed (1 session)} & 60 & 42 \\ \hline\text{Spaced (3 sessions)} & 60 & 71 \\ \hline\text{Spaced (6 sessions)} & 60 & 88 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which finding, if true, would most strengthen this hypothesis?
options: Participants who studied longer overall achieved higher retention regardless of schedule.
Memory reconsolidation occurs at fixed intervals that vary significantly between individuals.
Students prefer spaced schedules because they feel less mentally fatigued during study.
Retention scores rise with more frequent sessions despite identical total study time.
questions.explanation: Choice D directly supports the timing-optimization claim by showing improved retention with identical total time. Choice A contradicts the hypothesis by emphasizing total time over scheduling. Choice B introduces individual variability without linking it to the spacing effect. Choice C cites preference data, not retention outcomes.

questions.question:
Public health researchers claim that mandatory seat-belt laws reduce traffic fatalities primarily by preventing ejection from vehicles, not by reducing collision severity.

Which finding, if true, would most weaken this claim?
options: Ejection rates dropped by 82% in regions that adopted mandatory seat-belt legislation.
Vehicle safety ratings improved significantly during the same period as seat-belt law adoption.
Driver education programs emphasizing seat-belt use increased compliance rates by 34%.
Fatalities declined equally in low-speed and high-speed collisions following seat-belt law implementation.
questions.explanation: Choice D challenges the ejection-prevention mechanism by showing equal fatality reduction across collision speeds, suggesting a different protective factor. Choice A directly supports the ejection-prevention claim. Choice B introduces vehicle improvements, a confounding variable. Choice C addresses compliance, not the mechanism of protection.

questions.question:
Historians argue that the spread of printing technology accelerated scientific progress more by enabling rapid peer review than by increasing public literacy.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Region} & \text{Printing Presses (per 100k)} & \text{Scientific Publications/year} \\ \hline\text{Region X} & 2.1 & 14 \\ \hline\text{Region Y} & 5.8 & 47 \\ \hline\text{Region Z} & 12.3 & 132 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which finding, if true, would most strengthen this argument?
options: Public literacy rates increased steadily across all regions regardless of printing press density.
Regions with more printing presses showed faster citation cycles between scientific publications.
Scientific publications in Region Z focused primarily on theoretical rather than applied research.
Printing costs declined by 40% following technological improvements in paper manufacturing.
questions.explanation: Choice B directly supports the peer-review mechanism by linking press density to faster scholarly communication cycles. Choice A addresses literacy, which the argument explicitly downplays as the primary driver. Choice C discusses research focus, not dissemination speed. Choice D examines production costs, irrelevant to peer-review acceleration.

questions.question:
Marine biologists hypothesize that ocean acidification harms coral reef growth primarily by reducing calcium carbonate availability, not by increasing water temperature stress.

Which finding, if true, would most weaken this hypothesis?
options: Coral growth rates declined sharply in warm, acidic waters but remained stable in cool, acidic waters.
Calcium carbonate saturation levels dropped by 30% in regions experiencing rapid acidification.
Coral species with thicker skeletal structures showed greater resilience to pH changes across temperatures.
Laboratory experiments controlling for temperature still showed reduced calcification under low-pH conditions.
questions.explanation: Choice A challenges the calcium-carbonate mechanism by showing that temperature, not acidity alone, drives growth decline. Choice B directly supports the proposed mechanism. Choice C introduces skeletal structure, a biological variable not central to the hypothesis. Choice D supports the hypothesis by isolating pH effects from temperature.

questions.question:
Educational researchers claim that project-based learning improves student engagement primarily by increasing autonomy in task selection, not by enhancing peer collaboration opportunities.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Instructional Method} & \text{Autonomy Rating (1-10)} & \text{Engagement Score (%)} \\ \hline\text{Traditional Lecture} & 3.2 & 41 \\ \hline\text{Guided Projects} & 6.8 & 67 \\ \hline\text{Self-Directed Projects} & 9.1 & 89 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which finding, if true, would most strengthen this claim?
options: Students in self-directed projects reported higher satisfaction with group work than those in guided projects.
Engagement scores rise with autonomy ratings while peer interaction time remains constant across methods.
Traditional lecture courses achieved comparable engagement when instructors incorporated frequent class discussions.
Project-based learning requires more teacher preparation time than traditional instructional methods.
questions.explanation: Choice B directly supports the autonomy mechanism by showing engagement gains with constant peer interaction, isolating the proposed variable. Choice A emphasizes peer collaboration, which the claim explicitly downplays. Choice C introduces discussion frequency, a confounding instructional variable. Choice D addresses teacher workload, not student engagement mechanisms.

questions.question:
Urban planners propose that bike-lane expansions reduce traffic congestion primarily by shifting commuters from cars to bicycles, not by improving traffic flow for remaining vehicles.

Which finding, if true, would most weaken this proposal?
options: Bicycle commuting rates rose by 18% in neighborhoods following the installation of protected bike lanes.
Public transit usage declined slightly in regions that prioritized cycling infrastructure over bus expansions.
Average vehicle speeds increased by 22% on roads adjacent to new bike lanes without changes in car-commuter volume.
Traffic accident rates involving cyclists decreased significantly after protected lane implementation.
questions.explanation: Choice C challenges the mode-shift mechanism by showing congestion relief without reduced car-commuter volume, suggesting improved traffic flow instead. Choice A directly supports the proposed mode-shift mechanism. Choice B discusses transit substitution, not the car-to-bike shift central to the claim. Choice D addresses safety outcomes, not congestion reduction mechanisms.

questions.question:
Nutritionists claim that intermittent fasting improves metabolic health primarily by extending the daily fasting window, not by reducing total caloric intake.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Diet Protocol} & \text{Avg. Daily Fasting (hours)} & \text{Caloric Intake (kcal)} \\ \hline\text{Control (No Restriction)} & 12 & 2,100 \\ \hline\text{Time-Restricted (16:8)} & 16 & 1,950 \\ \hline\text{Alternate-Day Fasting} & 24 & 1,600 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which finding, if true, would most strengthen this claim?
options: Participants in the alternate-day fasting group reported greater hunger and difficulty adhering to the protocol.
Time-restricted eating led to greater weight loss than alternate-day fasting despite shorter fasting windows.
Caloric intake decreased significantly across all intervention groups compared to the control condition.
Metabolic markers improved proportionally with fasting duration even when caloric intake was held constant.
questions.explanation: Choice D directly supports the fasting-window mechanism by showing metabolic gains with constant calories, isolating the proposed variable. Choice A cites adherence difficulty, irrelevant to the physiological mechanism. Choice B compares weight-loss outcomes, not metabolic health markers. Choice C emphasizes caloric reduction, which the claim explicitly downplays.

questions.question:
Linguists hypothesize that bilingual children develop stronger executive function primarily by practicing language switching, not by enhanced exposure to diverse cultural perspectives.

Which finding, if true, would most weaken this hypothesis?
options: Monolingual children raised in multicultural environments showed executive function gains comparable to bilingual peers.
Bilingual children outperformed monolingual peers on tasks requiring rapid attention switching between rules.
Language-switching frequency correlated positively with executive function scores across bilingual participants.
Children who learned a second language after age 10 showed smaller executive function advantages than early bilinguals.
questions.explanation: Choice A challenges the language-switching mechanism by showing similar gains from cultural exposure alone, suggesting an alternative pathway. Choice B directly supports the switching-practice hypothesis. Choice C provides correlational evidence for the proposed mechanism. Choice D addresses age of acquisition, not the core mechanism comparison.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent work! You can accurately identify evidence that directly strengthens or weakens specific claims by evaluating mechanism alignment.
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review missed questions and focus on whether the evidence addresses the exact mechanism stated in the claim, not just a related topic.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Remember: eliminate options that are true but irrelevant, or that support a different aspect of the argument.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Ask yourself: does this finding prove or disprove the specific causal relationship the claim describes?
